nativeRange.habitat: Endemic to Australian coastal waters, ranging from southern Queensland around the southern coast to southwestern Western Australia, including Tasmania. Juveniles tend to occupy estuaries and shallow rocky reefs with seagrass; adults are found on inshore and offshore rocky reefs at depths reported between roughly 1 and 90 m.

diet.notes: In the wild the species has been documented feeding primarily on small crustaceans and worms taken from rocky-reef substrate. In captivity it is generally advised to offer a varied meaty diet of chopped marine fish flesh, scallops, shrimp/mysis, and other small crustacean preparations, supplemented occasionally with dried marine algae. Two small feedings per day is commonly recommended.

goodWith: peaceful temperate marine fish of similar size

summary: The Old Wife Fish is a distinctive temperate marine species endemic to southern Australian coastal reefs, easily recognised by its deep, laterally compressed body, two tall sickle-like dorsal fins, and 6–8 bold vertical black bars on a silver-white background. Care is generally considered intermediate, and the first dorsal spines are venomous.
careNotes: The Old Wife (Enoplosus armatus) is the sole living member of the family Enoplosidae and is endemic to southern Australia, found from southern Queensland around the south coast to southwestern Western Australia and Tasmania. In the wild it inhabits temperate inshore and offshore rocky reefs and has been documented occurring solitarily, in pairs, or in large schools. In captivity, single specimens, pairs, or small groups are all manageable, provided any multiple fish are introduced simultaneously to reduce conspecific tension. Tank-size recommendations vary across hobby sources, ranging from roughly 380 L to 950 L. Given the species' active nature and tendency to school, a minimum of around 700 L (approximately 185 US gal) is generally advised to allow adequate swimming room. The first dorsal-fin spines are venomous and have been compared to a bee sting in severity, so handling requires care. The Old Wife is generally considered not reef-safe: it will prey on small ornamental shrimp and other small mobile invertebrates and tends to cause damage in tanks with stony or soft corals. Juveniles and adults differ visibly — juveniles are more elongate, with blotched patterning and a white-rimmed spot on the soft dorsal fin, while adults develop the deep, laterally compressed body, two tall sickle-like dorsal fins, and the bold 6–8 vertical black bars on silver that make the species instantly recognisable. The common name is reported to derive from a tooth-grinding sound the fish produces when caught, said by early sailors to resemble the grumbling of an old wife. Availability in the hobby tends to be limited and unpredictable, and the Old Wife is best considered a rare specialty import.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Care sheet sourced via web search; the supplied collection URL (miscellaneous-fish) did not list this rare species but the dedicated product page was confirmed. Provides care level, tank size, pH, temperature, salinity, dKH, venomous status, and reef compatibility.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Enoplosidae, Centrarchiformes), maximum length (50 cm TL), endemicity to Australia, depth range (to 90 m), schooling/pairing behavior, venomous dorsal spines, and preferred temperature range (15.3–20.6°C, mean 17.7°C — cooler than LiveAquaria's recommendation; averaged with hobby sources to 18–24°C).

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Tertiary cross-check: 100 US gal minimum, 72–78°F (warmer than LiveAquaria/FishBase), pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, carnivorous diet (meaty foods twice daily), venomous dorsal spines, not reef-safe. Tank-size spread (100/180/250 gal) resolved by biasing larger to ~700 L per playbook rule for active reef fish needing swim room.

additional.name: Australian Museum
additional.notes: Authoritative range and biology: endemic from southern Queensland to southwestern Western Australia, common on coastal reefs as solitary individuals, pairs, or large schools; juveniles in estuaries and shallow seagrass/rocky-reef habitat. Typical adult size reported as ~25 cm (lower than FishBase's 50 cm maximum). Juvenile vs. adult color phase confirmed.

additional.name: The Aquarium Wiki
additional.notes: Designated secondary (Reef2Reef wiki) was not reachable; aquarium-wiki was used as a fourth hobby reference. Confirms 250 US gal (~946 L), 18–25°C, pH 8.0–8.5, SG 1.015–1.025, dKH 8–12, 5–12 year lifespan.

fish.breedingNotes: No reliable home-aquarium breeding protocol is documented. Spawning behavior in the wild appears to be poorly studied in the published hobby literature.
